food_subgroup: Soy-based protein foods — seasoned tofu dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tofu food product class (FOODON_03302757). No specific 'spicy tofu' subclass exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers soybean curd (tofu) generically. No spicy-seasoned variant code exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for spicy tofu dish identified. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared tofu dishes is limited.

preparation_method: served with spicy sauce or chili-based seasoning

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of spicy tofu is approximately 200 g, representing a standard restaurant portion as a side dish or light main in a Japanese ramen or izakaya context.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, raw, firm — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172443); adapted with spicy seasoning adjustment using USDA Foundation Foods soy sauce and chili paste components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy firm tofu (FDC ID 172443). Sodium and fat values adjusted upward to reflect typical spicy seasoning (soy sauce, chili paste, sesame oil) consistent with published composite analyses of seasoned tofu dishes. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a reasonable adaptation given no Singapore-specific or Japanese-government database entry for 'spicy tofu' as a prepared dish was identified in HPB Foodo or MEXT Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan (2020).

gi_source.title: Tofu — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for 'spicy tofu' as a prepared dish was found. GI of 15 is based on plain tofu as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care). Spicy seasoning (chili, soy sauce) does not materially alter the glycemic response of tofu given its very low carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (15 × 4.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.26, rounded to 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and chili seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Spicy tofu contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoning; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and high plant protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of 1.3 per serving, spicy tofu is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and may support glycemic control when consumed as a protein-rich alternative to higher-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favorable fatty acid profile and soy isoflavone content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spicy tofu provides predominantly polyunsaturated and monounsaturated fats with zero cholesterol, and soy protein has been associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction; however, the elevated sodium from seasoning warrants attention in cardiovascular disease management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy tofu is a Japanese-influenced prepared tofu dish consisting of firm or silken tofu seasoned with chili-based sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger, often garnished with green onion. As served at Japanese ramen restaurants such as Ramen Matsuri in Singapore, it typically appears as a side dish or appetizer alongside ramen. The texture ranges from silky and soft (silken tofu) to firm and slightly chewy depending on preparation. The flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, and moderately to intensely spicy. Nutritionally, spicy tofu is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with a very low glycemic index of approximately 15, making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking low-GI meal options. It provides approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, 7.8 g protein, 5.8 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 4.2 g carbohydrate. Calcium content is notable at approximately 200 mg per 100 g, supporting bone health. Sodium is elevated at approximately 380 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce and seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, and contains soy and sesame as primary allergens. Soy isoflavones present in tofu have been associated with modest cardiovascular benefits in epidemiological literature.
common_user_queries: How many calories in spicy tofu?
Is spicy tofu good for diabetics?
What is the GI of tofu?
Is spicy tofu high in sodium?
What protein does spicy tofu have?
Can I eat spicy tofu if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: Vegan protein option in Japanese cuisine

confidence_reason: No exact match for 'spicy tofu' as a prepared dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, MEXT Japan Standard Tables of Food Composition 2020, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy firm tofu (FDC ID 172443) with seasoning adjustments. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain tofu. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation is methodologically sound and the base source is a primary approved database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy firm tofu (FDC ID 172443) with upward sodium adjustment to reflect soy sauce and chili seasoning typical of Japanese spicy tofu preparations. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors (4-9-4 kcal/g for protein-fat-carbohydrate). 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for seasoned tofu preparations in the USDA SR Legacy source used. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tofu entry and tofu is not a significant dietary source of vitamin D unless fortified. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy tofu entry used. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; tofu and plant-based seasonings do not contain industrially produced trans fats. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; tofu is a plant-derived food containing no cholesterol. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; tofu is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12 unless fortified. 9. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on plain tofu from the University of Sydney GI Database; spicy seasoning does not materially alter glycemic response given the very low carbohydrate content. 10. The restaurant context is Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road, Singapore — a Japanese ramen restaurant; the dish is classified as Japanese cuisine. 11. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302757 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code for spicy tofu as a prepared dish was identified.
